coredata.dc:title: Increasing the migration and separation efficiencies of photogenerated carriers in CQDs/BiOCl through the point discharge effect
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Hailan
Zhang, Yushan
He, Shuaijun
Guan, Zeyu
Shi, Yintao
Xie, Xinyi
Xia, Dongsheng
Li, Dongya
Xu, Haiming
coredata.dc:description: Increasing the migration and separation efficiencies of photogenerated carriers can enormously enhance photocatalytic properties. Thus, a novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite with rich oxygen vacancies (OVs) was effectively synthesized via a hydrothermal method by using maltitol as a carbon source to address these issues. The photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, FT-IR, BET, XPS, PL, EIS, I-t, DRS and ESR. The experimental results demonstrate that BOC-1 can purify 72% tetracycline hydrochloride (10 mg/L, TC-HCl) within 60 min, which is 4 times higher than pure BiOCl. Moreover, photocatalytic mechanism was thoroughly studied and verified that the cooperation of point discharge effect (PDE) and rational OVs concentration induced by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can significantly enhance the migration and separation of photo-induced carriers, thereby leading to high photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared nanocomposites. In addition, the contribution of the free radicals was comprehensively calculated based on trapping experiments. the contributions of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 are 6.38%, 48.94% and 8.51%, respectively. This study provides a promising strategy to boosting the photoinduced carrier transfer properties by the coupling of point discharge effect and defect engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Separation efficiency
CQDs/BiOCl
Point discharge effect
Oxygen vacancy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51908432 ), the Central Committee Guides Local Science and Technology Development Project of Hubei Province, China (2019ZYYD068) and (ZYYD2020000027), Education and Teaching Reform Research Project of the Teaching Instruction Sub-committee of Water Supply and Drainage Engineering Major by Ministry of Education (GPSJZW2019-32).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the synthesized samples.
SEM images of the completed samples (a) BOC; (b) BOC-1; (c) BOC-2; (d) BOC-3; (e) BOC-4.
TEM images of the as-prepared BOC-1: (a) TEM; (b, c) HRTEM; (d) SAED.
FTIR spectra of a series of BOC materials prepared.
(a) UV–vis diffusion reflectance spectra of the synthesized samples and (b) transformed Kubelka-Munk functions vs. photon energy.
(a) Isothermal adsorption–desorption curves and (b) the corresponding pore-size distributions (inset) of BOC, BOC-1, BOC-2, BOC-3, and BOC-4.
ESR patterns of the synthesized samples.
XPS spectra of completed BOC-1: (a) C 1s and (b) O 1s.
PL spectra of the as-fabricated samples with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm.
(a) The EIS plots of the as-prepared samples, (b) the photocurrent curves of the as-prepared samples.
Mott-Schottky plots of BOC-1 and BOC at pH 7.
(a) The photocatalytic performance of synthesized samples for the degradation of TC-HCl under vis-light irradiation, (b) the corresponding pseudo first-order kinetics plots.
The DMPO-·O2 −, DMPO-·OH and TEMP-1O2 ESR spectra of BOC-1.
(a) Active species trapping experiments of BOC-1 for degrading TC-HCl; (b) Free radical contribution.
(a) The durability of BOC-1 for removes TC-HCl, (b) XRD patterns of BOC-1 before and after five cycles.
The proposed rational photocatalytic mechanism of BOC-1 under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Increasing the migration and separation efficiencies of photogenerated carriers in CQDs/BiOCl through the point discharge effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center Clean Production of Textile Dyeing and Printing, Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430073, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center Clean Production of Textile Dyeing and Printing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center Clean Production of Textile Dyeing and Printing, Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430073, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The OVs concentration of novel CQDs/BiOCl schottky heterojunction can be regulated via introduce CQDs. Therefore, the as-prepared sample present excellent photocatalytic performance due to the transformation efficiency of the photo-induced carriers was greatly enhanced under the coupling of rational OVs, point discharge effect, and interfacial electric field.

Increasing the migration and separation efficiencies of photogenerated carriers can enormously enhance photocatalytic properties. Thus, a novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite with rich oxygen vacancies (OVs) was effectively synthesized via a hydrothermal method by using maltitol as a carbon source to address these issues. The photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, FT-IR, BET, XPS, PL, EIS, I-t, DRS and ESR. The experimental results demonstrate that BOC-1 can purify 72% tetracycline hydrochloride (10 mg/L, TC-HCl) within 60 min, which is 4 times higher than pure BiOCl. Moreover, photocatalytic mechanism was thoroughly studied and verified that the cooperation of point discharge effect (PDE) and rational OVs concentration induced by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can significantly enhance the migration and separation of photo-induced carriers, thereby leading to high photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared nanocomposites. In addition, the contribution of the free radicals was comprehensively calculated based on trapping experiments. the contributions of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 are 6.38%, 48.94% and 8.51%, respectively. This study provides a promising strategy to boosting the photoinduced carrier transfer properties by the coupling of point discharge effect and defect engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: As a defect point, OVs can not only promote the migration of photogenerated electrons from the valence band maximum (VBM) to the conduction band minimum (CBM) and mend the light-harvesting efficiency, but can also serve as electron traps to limit the recombination of photogenerated carriers and lengthen their lifetimes [1]. Moreover, abundant dangling bonds that are attracted by OVs can act as catalytic centres and adsorption sites [2]. Therefore, many researchers have sought to improve photocatalytic performance via the use of OVs.

For BiOCl, OVs can easily be produced due to its special crystal structure and physicochemical properties, and thus dramatically raise the photocatalytic activity. However, the poor migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers in BiOCl severely impede their wide utilization. Therefore, various methods such as doping [3], defect engineering [4], facet engineering [5], microstructure adjustment [6] and constructing heterostructures [7] were used to enhance the catalytic performance. Especially, constructing heterostructure with rational OVs received tremendous interest because the photogenerated e−-h+ pairs with superior separation efficiency under the coupling of OVs and the interfacial electric field (IEF). Therefore, BiOCl/Bi2O3 [8], WO3/BiOCl [9], La(OH)3/BiOCl [10] and MoS2/BiOCl [11] exhibit outstanding photocatalytic properties. However, excess OVs in nanocomposites will act as the recombination centres and seriously impact the photocatalytic performance [12]. Thus, the combination of non-metal materials with BiOCl to regulate the OVs concentration also increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and has attracted increasing attention.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are composed of sp2/sp3 carbon clusters with amorphous or nanocrystalline structures, and its diameters are typically less than 10 nm [13]. As a new class of carbon nanomaterials, CQDs present many merits, such as low toxicity, chemical inertness, excellent electron transfer properties and suitable band structure [14]. Furthermore, CQDs can act as electron reservoirs and extract photoinduced electrons from the conduction bands (CBs) of semiconductors when they formed Schottky heterojunctions [15]. In addition, the OVs can be modulated by forming C-O bonds at the interface of CQDs and metal oxides. Thereby, CQDs/Bi2WO6 [16], CQDs/ZnIn2S4 [17] and CQDs/BiOCl [18] all are reveals eminent photocatalytic effect due to the superior spatial separation results of photogenerated carriers. Although these studies have made significant progress, few studies have focused on point discharge effect (PDE) to ameliorate photocatalytic properties. PDE is a type of corona discharge. At the tip of a conductor, air molecules ionize to form many free electrons and ions due to the dense charge and intense electric field [19]. Moreover, the periodic crystal structure was seriously destructed at the tip and produces many dangling bonds that are induced by the unsaturated coordination atoms. Thus, many substrate molecules will adsorb at the convex tip and active via the accumulated carriers at the convex tip. Eventually, the photocatalytic performance is significantly improved.

Herein, a novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite with moderate OVs was successfully prepared by using maltitol as a carbon source. The photocatalysts were characterized using different analysis methods. The experimental results demonstrate that the CQDs/BiOCl heterojunction can purify 72% tetracycline hydrochloride (10 mg/L, TC-HCl) within 60 min, which is 4 times faster than pure BiOCl. Additionally, the suggested mechanism of the TC degradation process using the optimum photocatalyst was suggested. The photocatalysis principle research unraveled that the optimal OVs concentration that was induced by the CQDs and the PDE significantly improved the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, the as-prepared sample exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the contribution of the free radicals to the photocatalytic properties was thoroughly investigated based on free radical inhibition experiments and the ESR technique. The synergy of PDE, defect engineering and heterojunctions provides new strategies for increasing catalytic activity. This study supplies a promising approach for increasing the transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers through the synergy of PDE and defect engineering.

All chemical reagents were analytical grade. Bismuth(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Sodium chloride (NaCl), P-benzoquinone (PBQ), T-butyl alcohol (TBA) and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Maltitol, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl), L(+)-histidine (L-His), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were furnished by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

In the typical process, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and disparate quantities of maltitol (0, 100, 300, 500, and 1000 mg) were injected into 25 mL ultrapure water at a normal temperature. Next, this solution was processed for 20 min through an ultrasonic machine, 5 mL of KCl (0.2 mmol/L) was injected into it through a syringe, and the pH value of the liquid was adjusted to 2 using KOH and HNO3. Afterward, the above solution was stored in a 50 mL autoclave, in which it was heated to 160 °C for 24 h. Then, the prepared sample was cooled to room temperature, washed several times in deionized water and dried for 12 h under vacuum conditions at 60℃. The samples were labelled as BOC, BOC-1, BOC-2, BOC-3 and BOC-4, which contained various concentrations of maltitol (0, 100, 300, 500, and 1000 mg, respectively).

The XRD patterns of the successfully prepared samples were displayed using an X-ray powder diffraction diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (1.54 Å, 40 kV, 40 mA) within 2θ range of 10° to 80°. The morphology and microstructure of samples were recorded by a Hitachi S4800 FEG SEM and Tecnai G2 20 TEM. The surface groups of samples were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using a NICOLET IS5 spectrometer. The light absorption characteristics of the samples were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Japan Hitachi U4100) utilizing BaSO4 as a reflection reference. The pore size and surface area of each sample were characterized through N2 adsorption–desorption on a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 (USA) instrument. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was performed using a BRUKER A300 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. A SHIMADZU RF-6000 instrument was employed to obtain the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted, photocurrent spectra (I-t) and Mote Schottky plots (Mott) were obtained using an electrochemistry workstation (PEC 1000, Perfect Light).

The photocatalytic performance of catalytic material was assessed via the removal of TC-HCl solution (10 mg/L) using an LED lamp (50 W, λ≧420 nm). 25 mg as-synthesized sample was dispersed in 50 mL TC-HCl solution (10 mg/L) and processed by ultrasonic machine for 5 min (Fig. S1). The mixture solution was uniformly stirred 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium in darkness before turning on the LED lamp. The concentration of TC-HCl was monitored by a METASH UV-5100B spectrophotometer at 358 nm in the same intervals. After the conversion of absorbance and concentration, removal efficiency was calculated by formula, detailed calculation is shown in Formula S1.

The phase structures and crystallinity of the catalytic material were examined via XRD (Fig. 1 ). The diffraction peaks of the crystal samples were all accurately indexed to BiOCl (JCPDS: 06–0249), thereby demonstrating the satisfactory crystallinity and the high purity of the products. However, the diffraction intensities of the (0 0 1), (0 0 2), (1 0 2) and (1 1 1) facets were significantly reduced with increasing amounts of maltitol due to the oriented growth that was induced by maltitol. The relative crystallinity also decreases (Table S1). In addition, no typical diffraction peaks of CQDs were observed due to their low content or extensive dispersion in CQDs/BiOCl (Fig. S2) [20,21].

The morphology and microstructure of completed samples were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, pure BiOCl consisted mainly of irregular nanosheets that were approximately 56 nm thick. Moreover, according to careful observation, convex nanoparticles were dispersed on the nanosheets. However, the nanosheets self-assembled into flower-like microspheres under the controlling of maltitol. Meanwhile, the thickness of the nanosheets was reduced from 38 nm to 15 nm with increasing maltitol amount (Fig. 2b-e). However, no CQDs could be clearly observed in the SEM images. Thus, BOC-1 was selected for further investigation of its microstructure using TEM.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, many light tip points are embedded in the surface of the nanosheet. According to further observation, these tip points, which have diameters of less than 5 nm and lattice spacing (0.275 nm), fully correspond to the (1 1 0) facet of BiOCl (Fig. 3b, c). Moreover, it is well-established that these tip points can easily produce PDE and can promote the preferential accumulation of carriers at the tip points, and thus the migration and separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers will be enormously enhanced [22]. In addition, some amorphous areas with diameters less than 5 nm (white ellipse circles) are well dispersed on the nanosheets which may result from the carbonization of maltitol during the synthesis process (Fig. 3b, c). Moreover, the regular diffraction spots in Fig. 3c demonstrate that BOC-1 is a single crystal. As shown in Fig. S3, the well-distributed elements Bi, O, Cl and C were found in CQDs/BiOCl. Therefore, the novel nanocomposite was successfully prepared according to the aforementioned analysis.

The CQD surface always contains many functional groups (e.g., –COOH and –OH), which significantly affect its optical properties [23]. Therefore, pure CQDs were prepared under the same conditions without KCl and Bi(NO3)3 and characterized via XRD (Fig. S2) and FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 4 , the signals center at 3422 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 were result from the surface hydroxyl groups and adsorbed water molecules, respectively [24]. Moreover, the peak at 2855 cm−1 was originated from the residual maltitol because it was not thoroughly carbonized in BOC-3 and BOC-4 and, thus leads to the C-H bond vibration [16]. In contrast, the maltitol in BOC-1 and BOC-2 was completely carbonized, and thus, no C-H bond vibration can be detected. The signal at 1704 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C = O bond vibration of CQDs [25]. Besides, according to careful observation, the intensity of the C = O bond was gradually increased because more maltitol was carbonized to CQDs as its amount increased. The bond length of the C = O bond (0.12 nm) is shorter than Bi-O (0.1934 nm) while the bond energy of C = O (755.6 kJ/mol) exceeds that of Bi-O (337.2 kJ/mol) [26,27]. Therefore, CQDs can wrest oxygen atoms from BiOCl through forming C = O bonds at the interface of CQDs/BiOCl and can regulate the OVs concentration. Additionally, the signal at 1384 cm-1 was induced by the asymmetric vibration of Bi-Cl [28]. Simultaneously, we can observed that the peak of the Bi-O bond at 570 cm−1 in the nanocomposites gradually shifted to a lower wavenumber with increasing maltitol concentration due to the formation of new chemical bonds at the interface of CQDs/BiOCl [29]. Therefore, according to this analysis, we undoubtedly conclude that the novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite was successfully synthesized.

The optical absorption characteristics of the completed samples were investigated via UV–vis DRS. In Fig. 5 a, the absorption edges of pure BiOCl and BOC-1 are at approximately 372 nm. In addition, the colour of the as-prepared samples was change from white (pristine BiOCl) to caramel (BOC-4) because more CQDs were formed with increasing the concentrations of maltitol. Thus, the absorption edges of BOC-2, BOC-3 and BOC-4 were significantly expanded to infrared region. Moreover, according to careful observation, all the samples have additional exponential absorption tails in the Vis-light range, which may be induced by the OVs [30]. In addition, the band gaps of BOC-1 (3.33 eV) and BOC-4 (2.30 eV) were calculated based on the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. 5b and Table S1).

The specific area and pore size of the as-prepared samples were determined via the nitrogen adsorption–desorption technique. As presented in Fig. 6 a and Tab. S2, the surface areas of the samples were 18.6835, 11.1346, 18.8740, 23.6962 and 25.8016 m2/g, respectively. Samples with large specific areas usually show superior photocatalytic activity because they can offer more adsorption sites and active centres [31]. Thus, the normalized apparent rate constant (ks ) was adopted to comprehensively examine how the specific area affects the photocatalytic character. As presented in Tab. S1, BOC-1 has the largest value of ks (3.067914 × 10−3 min−1 L m−2), while it shows the smallest surface area; thus, the specific surface area is not the decisive factor for the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, all samples are present type III adsorption–desorption isotherms and H3 hysteresis. Simultaneously, the pore sizes are mainly 20–30 nm; hence, the most prominent pore structure in the as-prepared samples is a mesoporous structure (Fig. 6b).

In order to research CQDs how to affect the OVs concentration in the as-samples, the ESR technique was utilized. From Fig. 7 , the OVs concentration gradually raised with increasing amounts of maltitol because more maltitol was carbonized to CQDs. Therefore, CQDs can capture many oxygen atoms in BiOCl by forming C = O bonds at the interface of CQDs/BiOCl and produce abundant OVs; thus, the OVs concentration in CQDs/BiOCl can be modulated by introducing CQDs. Rational OVs can enhance light utilization and boost the separation of photogenerated carriers [32]. Therefore, the as-prepared samples may exhibit outstanding photocatalytic performance.

The surface composition and chemical states of BOC-1 were identified via XPS. The spectra were calibrated based on the binding energy of C 1 s at 284.6 eV. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. S4a demonstrates that BOC-1 is consisted of Bi, O, Cl, and C. In addition, the binding energies at 159.4 and 164.7 eV can be attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2; thus, bismuth is present mainly as Bi3+ in BOC-1 (Fig. S4b) [33]. The two major peaks at 198.1 eV and 199.6 eV in Fig. S4c correspond to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively [34]. In addition, the C 1 s peak can be deconvolved into three peaks at 284.8, 285.8 and 288.2 eV (Fig. 8 a). The main peak fitted at 284.8 eV originates from the sp2 orbital hybridization of the C-C bond [18]. The other peaks were situated at 285.8 [35] and 288.2 eV [36] and originated from C-O-C and C = O, respectively. Fig. S4(d-f) showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, which revealed the surface functional groups on the CQDs. The result of XPS analysis indicated the coexistence of BiOCl and CQDs in the CQDs/BiOCl materials. In addition, the peaks that are centred at 529.9, 531.3 and 533.1 eV can be attributed to lattice oxygen, surface adsorbed hydroxyl groups and OVs that are induced by CQDs, respectively (Fig. 8b) [1]. The results of this analysis further demonstrated that the novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite was successfully fabricated and coincided with the characterization results of TEM and FT-IR.

The photoluminescence (PL) technique was employed to investigate the conversion and recombination behaviors of the photoinduced carriers. As shown in Fig. 9 , pristine BOC with a strong PL intensity at 543 nm was identified based on its properties. Meanwhile, the PL intensity was increased with increasing maltitol concentration because abundant OVs were formed due to more maltitol carbonizing to CQDs. In addition, it is well accepted that excess OVs will act as recombination centres of photoinduced carriers, thereby leading to BOC-4 having the strongest PL signal and BOC-1 having the weakest PL signal [12]. This result also demonstrates that BOC-1 has superior separation and migration and high efficiency of photogenerated carriers and may present excellent photocatalytic activities.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the transfer resistance of photoinduced carriers. As displayed in Fig. 10 a, BOC-1 has the lowest arc radius in all the samples; hence, BOC-1 has the lowest transfer resistance among photogenerated carriers [37]. Moreover, the photocurrent was further employed to examine the charge transfer efficiencies. As shown in Fig. 10b, the photocurrents of the nanocomposites all exceed that of pure BiOCl due to the satisfactory electron conductivity of CQDs. However, more CQDs will produce a light shielding effect [38] and significantly reduce the light utilization efficiencies of BOC-3 and BOC-4; thus, BOC-1 and BOC-2 have the largest photocurrents.

The Mott-Schottky plots of BOC and BOC-1 were revealed in Fig. 11 . The positively sloping reflect that BiOCl and BOC-1 are n-type semiconductors [39]. Furthermore, the flat band potentials of BOC and BOC-1 are approximately −0.68 V and −0.61 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, pH = 7). The conduction band potential is usually 0.2 eV less [40] than the flat band potential; thus, the conduction band potentials of BOC and BOC-1 when transferred to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) are approximately −0.68 V (vs. NHE, pH = 7) and −0.61 V (vs. NHE, pH = 7), respectively, based on the following expression. Meanwhile, according to the band gaps (E g) of BOC and BOC-1, the CB/VB potentials of BOC and BOC-1 are −0.68/2.53 V and −0.61/2.72 V (vs. NHE, pH = 7), respectively.
E NHE = E Ag/AgCl + E 0 Ag/AgCl E 0 Ag/AgCl = 0.197 V

The photocatalytic performances of the CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposites were evaluated via elimination 10 mg/L TC-HCl (Tab. S3). As shown in Fig. 12 a, the self-degradation experiment showed that TC-HCl can exist stably under visible light irradiation. Moreover, BOC-1 can purify 72% TC-HCl (10 mg/L) within 60 min, which is 4 times faster than pure BiOCl. This result mainly stems from BOC-1 having rational OVs and having the highest transformation efficiency of photogenerated carriers; thus, the photocatalytic properties of BOC-1 were tremendously enhanced. Fig. S5 shows the graph of the analysis pHpzc of the BOC-1 photocatalyst, which was evaluated by the pH drift method [7]. The pHpzc of the BOC-1 was about 5 which means at pH values lower than 5, the surface of photocatalyst will charged positively while at higher pH value it will charged negatively. However, the photocatalytic activities of BOC-2, BOC-3 and BOC-4 were severely reduced because more CQDs were covered on BiOCl, which severely reduced the light utilization efficiency due to the light shielding effect [41]. Moreover, excess OVs that were induced by abundant CQDs became the recombination centres of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and finally lead to poor photocatalytic properties of BOC-2, BOC-3 and BOC-4. In addition, according to the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetics of the samples (Fig. 12b), BOC-1 has the largest apparent rate constant (0.01186 min−1), which further demonstrates that BOC-1 has outstanding photocatalytic activity. The degradation pathway of tetracycline hydrochloride has been briefly analyzed by LC-MS (Fig. S6). The main transformation products of TC-HCl are dealkylation, hydroxylation, and benzene ring cleavage [42]. Compared with other materials that degrade TC-HCl, BOC-1 has a relatively better degradation effect in this study (Tab. S6).

The reactive oxygen species (ROSs) in the BOC-1/Vis system were characterized by ESR technique. As reflect in Fig. 13 , the signals of DMPO-·O2 −, DMPO-·OH and TEMP-1O2 adducts are all detected; indicating ·O2 −, ·OH and 1O2 are all present in the BOC-1/Vis system. In addition, according to careful observation, the signal of the DMPO-·O2 − adduct was stronger than that of DMPO-·OH and TEMP-1O2, thereby suggesting that ·O2 − may be the dominant free radical in the BOC-1/Vis system.

To further investigate the roles of ROSs in the BOC-1/Vis system, PBQ, TBA, EDTA-2Na, and L-histidine (L-his) were used as the scavengers of ·O2 −, ·OH, h+, and 1O2 [43]. As shown in Fig. 14 a, the photocatalytic performance was severely hindered when introduce PBQ, illustrating ·O2 − plays a key role in the catalytic process. However, the photocatalytic properties changed slightly when TBA and L-His were added, thereby implying that ·OH and 1O2 have small impacts on the photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic activity was reduced again when EDTA-2Na was introduced; thus, h+ is the dominant active species in the BOC-1/Vis system. These results match well with the ESR characterization analysis (Fig. 13) [44]. In addition, the contributions of the free radicals to the photocatalytic performance were calculated (the calculations are presented in detail in Tab. S3 and 4). As shown in Fig. 14b, the contributions of ·OH, ·O2 − and 1O2 to the photocatalytic performance are 6.38%, 48.94% and 8.51%, respectively [45]. In addition, the contribution of the nonradical process (h+ and/or e−) to the photocatalytic activity is 36.17%. Therefore, according to these results, we conclude that TC-HCl in the BOC-1/Vis system was purified mainly by ·O2 − and the nonradical process.

On the basis of the results and analysis that are presented above, the photocatalytic principle of the sample was identified and is presented in Scheme 1 . Above all, rational OVs can benefit the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers [32]. Excess OVs will turn into the recombination centres of photogenerated carriers, while few OVs will significantly reduce the solar energy utilization efficiency [1]. Moreover, the C = O bond (0.12 nm) is shorter than the Bi-O bond (0.1934 nm), while the bond energy of the C = O bond (755.6 kJ/mol) exceeds that of the Bi-O bond (337.2 kJ/mol). Therefore, CQDs that originate from the carbonization of maltitol can wrest oxygen atoms from BiOCl by forming C = O bonds and, finally, modulate the OV concentration, which substantially improves the separation and migration of the photoinduced carriers. However, BOC-2, BOC-3 and BOC-4 present poor photocatalytic performance because CQDs that are covered on BiOCl will produce a light shielding effect and excess OVs [39].

Second, IEF promotes the spatial transformation of photoinduced carriers. The Schottky heterojunction between CQDs and BiOCl was successfully constructed due to their suitable band structure. Furthermore, CQDs can serve as electron reservoirs to extract many electrons from other materials when they form Schottky heterostructures [15]. Therefore, abundant photoinduced electrons that accumulate in the CB of BiOCl will rapidly transfer to CQDs under the drive of IEF. Meanwhile, many photogenerated holes will leave in the VB of BiOCl, thereby hindering the recombination of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs.

Third, the PDE stems from the novel composites can boost the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers [19]. Abundant photoinduced holes accumulated at the tip points due to the PDE when many photogenerated electrons migrate to CQDs driven under the drive of IEF. Therefore, the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers was tremendously augmented. In addition, although the VB potential of BiOCl is more positive than that of E(·OH/OH− = +1.99 V), a small amount of ·OH was produced in the present system because the oxygenation capability was limited by the quantum size effect of CQDs. Therefore, many photoinduced holes directly degraded TC-HCl via a nonradical pathway. In addition, many photoinduced electrons that accumulated in CQDs reduced the amount of dissolved and/or adsorbed oxygen for the generation of abundant ·O2 −. Eventually, the transformation efficiency of the photoinduced carriers was tremendously increased under the cooperation of rational OVs, PDE and IEF, and, thus, TC-HCl was purified mainly by ·O2 − and the nonradical process.

A cycle experiment was executed to investigate the durability and stability of BOC-1. As shown in Fig. 15 a, although the photocatalytic performance was reduced after 5 cycles due to the loss of catalytic activity, the sample still showed satisfactory durability. In addition, the XRD patterns of BOC-1 after 5 cycles has no any changes compared with the pristine BiOCl (Fig. 15b), thereby indicating that the CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite is highly stable during the cycling process.

A novel CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposite with rich OVs was fabricated via a hydrothermal method using maltitol as a carbon source. Photocatalytic experiments show that the CQDs/BiOCl heterojunction can degrade 72% TC-HCl within 60 min, which is 4 times more than pristine BiOCl. And the contributions of ·OH, ·O2 − and 1O2 to the photocatalytic performance are 6.38%, 48.94% and 8.51%, respectively. In addition, addition results demonstrated that the light utilization efficiency and the separation of photogenerated carriers can be enormously enhanced by modulating the OV concentration through the introduction of CQDs. IEF and PDE also promote the spatial transformation of photoinduced carriers and effectively hinder their recombination. Thus, CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposites present outstanding photocatalytic activity under the coupling of rational OVs, IEF and PDE. Moreover, the contribution of free radicals to the photocatalytic performance was comprehensively investigated via trapping experiments and ESR techniques. This study offers not only a fresh strategy for regulating OVs by introducing CQDs but also a promising method for increasing the transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers via the synergy of PDE and defect engineering.

Hailan Qin: Validation, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yushan Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Shuaijun He: Data curation. Zeyu Guan: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yintao Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xinyi Xie: Validation, Investigation. Dongsheng Xia: Supervision, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Dongya Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Haiming Xu: Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51908432), the Central Committee Guides Local Science and Technology Development Project of Hubei Province, China (2019ZYYD068) and (ZYYD2020000027), Education and Teaching Reform Research Project of the Teaching Instruction Sub-committee of Water Supply and Drainage Engineering Major by Ministry of Education (GPSJZW2019-32).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.150214.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
